feedback_text:
DIAGNOSIS: 40 failing sub-attributes (85.7 pts recoverable), 0 passing. 9 regressions detected — recovery is top priority.

==================================================
REGRESSIONS (RECOVER THESE FIRST — highest priority):
==================================================

  REGRESSED: organizational_vendor_alignment — was 69% at iter 2, now 0% (-69%)
    What was lost: The best version contained specific vendor management complexity matching through regulatory compliance integration (AICPA AT-C sections 105 and 205), realistic resource-procedure consistency with detailed scope definitions that matched startup capabilities, and appropriate change capacity considerations through startup-optimized remediation strategies. It also included specific regulatory frameworks (GDPR Article 28, CCPA) that demonstrated governance maturity alignment.
    Why it dropped: The current version expanded the scope definition with excessive technical detail and complexity that mismatches a 50-person startup's governance maturity (adding CyberArk, EKS, Terraform, ELK stack, Transit Gateway, etc.), removed the regulatory compliance integration section that provided governance-vendor alignment, and eliminated the startup-specific optimization language that ensured resource-procedure consistency and change capacity considerations.
    → RECOVER: Restore the regulatory compliance integration section with AICPA AT-C references and management assertion development, simplify the scope definition to remove enterprise-level tools inappropriate for a 50-person startup (remove CyberArk, EKS, Terraform, ELK stack, Transit Gateway), and re-add explicit 'startup-optimized' language throughout to ensure vendor procedures match organizational resources and change capacity.

  REGRESSED: auditor_selection_timing — was 60% at iter 2, now 0% (-60%)
    What was lost: The best version (iteration 2) contained no auditor selection analysis, timing strategy, or customer acceptance requirements content either - both versions are incomplete excerpts that cut off before reaching any auditor selection content. The regression appears to be in the scope definition section becoming more verbose and detailed in the current version.
    Why it dropped: The current version expanded the scope definition with excessive detail and longer descriptions, but this expansion may have pushed the auditor selection content further down or out of the excerpt entirely. The content became more verbose without adding the required auditor selection analysis components.
    → RECOVER: Add a dedicated 'Auditor Selection Strategy' section immediately after the scope definition that includes: (1) Analysis of Big Four vs national vs regional audit firms with specific cost ranges ($15K-50K for regional, $50K-150K for national, $150K+ for Big Four), (2) Enterprise customer acceptance requirements research showing Big Four preference for Fortune 500 clients, (3) Engagement timing strategy recommending Q2-Q3 start for cost optimization and auditor availability, and (4) Market credibility implications for B2B SaaS client acquisition.

  REGRESSED: evidence_control_integration — was 59% at iter 2, now 0% (-59%)
    What was lost: The best version contained critical evidence-control integration elements that were completely removed: (1) Specific regulatory compliance integration section with AICPA AT-C section 105 and 205 references that directly linked audit evidence requirements to control design, (2) Management Assertion Development framework (AT-C 205.35) that explicitly connected control procedures to auditor evidence collection, (3) Clear linkage between Trust Service Criteria selection and specific evidence generation requirements (e.g., GDPR Article 28 processor requirements for Privacy TSC), and (4) Explicit statement about how optional TSCs impact evidence collection with auditor guidance.
    Why it dropped: The current version expanded the scope definition with extensive technical details but completely eliminated the regulatory compliance integration section that provided the critical bridge between control design and evidence generation. The removal of AICPA attestation standard references eliminated the explicit connection between control procedures and auditor testing approaches. The current version focuses on system inventory rather than how these systems will generate appropriate audit evidence, losing the evidence-control alignment that made the best version score well.
    → RECOVER: Immediately after the Trust Service Criteria Selection section, restore the complete 'Regulatory Compliance Integration' section from the best version, including all AICPA AT-C references and the Management Assertion Development framework. Then add a new subsection called 'Evidence Generation Framework' that explicitly maps each in-scope system to its evidence collection procedures and explains how the detailed system inventory will support specific auditor testing requirements. Maintain the expanded technical scope details but ensure each system description includes how it will generate audit evidence.

  REGRESSED: control_remediation_coherence — was 56% at iter 2, now 0% (-56%)
    What was lost: The best version contained critical remediation planning elements including: (1) Clear Trust Service Criteria selection rationale with specific business justifications (e.g., 'Recommended for SaaS with uptime SLAs ≥99.5%'), (2) Regulatory compliance integration section with specific AICPA AT-C citations and management assertion development framework, and (3) The beginning of a structured remediation approach that would have included priority timelines, resource allocation, and cross-criteria dependencies.
    Why it dropped: The current version expanded the scope definition with excessive technical detail but completely eliminated the remediation coherence framework. It removed the Trust Service Criteria selection rationale, deleted the regulatory compliance integration section, and cut off before reaching any remediation planning content. The focus shifted from remediation strategy to technical inventory, losing all elements that would demonstrate priority_timeline_alignment, resource_gap_consistency, and cross_criteria_dependencies.
    → RECOVER: Restore the complete Trust Service Criteria selection section with business justifications, add back the full Regulatory Compliance Integration section with AICPA AT-C citations, and continue the document to include: (1) A priority matrix mapping highest-risk gaps to remediation timelines, (2) Resource allocation framework showing FTE requirements and budget constraints for a 50-person startup, and (3) Cross-criteria dependency mapping showing how vendor management controls affect both security and availability TSCs. Keep the enhanced scope definition but ensure it leads to actionable remediation planning.

  REGRESSED: timeline_resource_feasibility — was 60% at iter 2, now 34% (-26%)
    What was lost: The best version (iteration 2) contained the beginning of a structured timeline and resource planning framework, including detailed scope definition with specific technical components, regulatory compliance integration addressing AICPA AT-C requirements, and management assertion development. It established a foundation for comprehensive timeline planning that accounted for SOC 2 examination phases.
    Why it dropped: The current version (iteration 3) expanded the scope definition with excessive technical detail but completely removed any timeline planning content, resource allocation framework, and contingency planning elements. While it provided more granular system descriptions, it eliminated the critical timeline and resource feasibility components that the criterion measures, resulting in zero coverage of phase timeline accuracy, cash flow alignment, and contingency buffer planning.
    → RECOVER: Retain the enhanced technical scope detail from the current version but immediately add a comprehensive 'Implementation Timeline & Resource Planning' section that includes: (1) Month-by-month SOC 2 timeline covering 3-6 month description period, 6-12 month testing period, and auditor busy season constraints; (2) Phased budget structure with startup cash flow considerations and cost optimization strategies; (3) 15-25% contingency buffer planning with specific delay scenarios for common SOC 2 implementation challenges. Place this section immediately after the scope definition to maintain the improved technical detail while recovering the timeline and resource planning framework.

  REGRESSED: regulatory_attestation_compliance — was 94% at iter 2, now 70% (-24%)
    What was lost: The best version contained specific regulatory compliance citations and framework development that was truncated in the current version. Specifically lost: (1) Complete AICPA AT-C section 105 and 205 citations with specific requirements, (2) AT-C section 205.35 management assertion development framework that was cut off mid-sentence, (3) GDPR Article 28 processor requirements integration, (4) TSP Section 100 requirements for description criteria, and (5) The structured approach to professional attestation standards compliance.
    Why it dropped: The current version appears to have been truncated during generation, cutting off critical regulatory compliance content. While it expanded the scope definition with more detailed technical descriptions, it lost the essential regulatory framework citations and management assertion development that made the best version compliant with AT-C 205.35 requirements. The content became more technically detailed but less professionally compliant with attestation standards.
    → RECOVER: Restore the complete regulatory compliance section from the best version starting with 'Management Assertion Development (AT-C 205.35):' and continue with the full framework. Add back the specific AICPA AT-C section 105 and 205 citations, GDPR Article 28 references, and TSP Section 100 requirements. Ensure the management assertion requirements are fully developed per AT-C 205.35 compliance, including system description criteria per TSP Section 100.73. Keep the enhanced technical scope details from the current version but ensure regulatory citations are complete and not truncated.

  REGRESSED: control_operational_effectiveness — was 66% at iter 2, now 50% (-16%)
    What was lost: The best version had a more focused and structured approach with clear scope boundaries, specific system categorization, and comprehensive regulatory compliance integration including AICPA AT-C section requirements and management assertion development. It maintained concise, actionable content that could more easily accommodate detailed automation identification, exception handling procedures, and scalability considerations in subsequent sections.
    Why it dropped: The current version expanded the scope definition with excessive granular detail (e.g., listing specific AWS services, container orchestration, CyberArk, ELK stack) that consumed content space without adding operational value. This verbose approach left insufficient room for the critical operational effectiveness content - comprehensive automation recommendations across 60%+ of controls, detailed exception handling procedures for all control areas, and explicit scalability planning for growth scenarios.
    → RECOVER: Revert to the best version's concise scope definition format while retaining any genuinely valuable additions from the current version. Then allocate the recovered content space to: 1) Add specific automation tooling recommendations for 15+ manual controls across all TSC areas (access reviews, log monitoring, vulnerability scanning, backup verification, etc.), 2) Include comprehensive exception handling procedures for each critical control area with escalation paths and approval workflows, and 3) Explicitly address how each control design will scale from 50 to 200+ employees with specific growth scenario planning.

  REGRESSED: risk_impact_prioritization — was 76% at iter 2, now 60% (-16%)
    What was lost: The best version contained a structured quantitative risk methodology framework with weighted factors (business impact, implementation complexity, auditor focus, customer requirements, regulatory impact), detailed remediation sequencing with dependency mapping, and explicit materiality understanding with auditor perspective considerations. It also included specific regulatory compliance integration addressing AICPA AT-C sections and management assertion development requirements.
    Why it dropped: The current version expanded the scope definition with excessive detail but removed the quantitative risk scoring framework, eliminated the remediation roadmap with dependency mapping, and lost the materiality thresholds and auditor perspective considerations. The content became more descriptive of systems rather than analytical of risks and remediation strategies.
    → RECOVER: Restore the quantitative risk methodology section with 5+ weighted factors and numerical scoring model applied to 20+ identified gaps. Add back the detailed remediation roadmap with dependency mapping and resource allocation for the 50-person organization. Include explicit materiality thresholds for startup scale with auditor perspective considerations. Keep the enhanced scope definition but follow it immediately with the risk assessment framework and remediation sequencing that was present in the best version.

  REGRESSED: governance_maturity_assessment — was 41% at iter 2, now 35% (-6%)
    What was lost: The best version included critical governance framework elements: (1) Trust Service Criteria selection with detailed justification for each criterion (Security, Availability, Processing Integrity, Confidentiality, Privacy), (2) Regulatory compliance integration section addressing AICPA AT-C sections 105 and 205 with specific attestation requirements, (3) Management assertion development framework per AT-C 205.35, and (4) structured methodology for governance domain assessment. These elements provided the foundation for comprehensive governance maturity assessment, policy framework design, and change management capability evaluation.
    Why it dropped: The current version replaced the structured governance framework with expanded technical infrastructure details. It removed the Trust Service Criteria analysis, eliminated the regulatory compliance integration section, and cut the management assertion development content. Instead of building upon the governance foundation, it focused heavily on technical scope expansion (adding more infrastructure details, business systems, network components) without maintaining the governance maturity assessment framework that was scoring well in the previous iteration.
    → RECOVER: Restore the complete 'Trust Service Criteria Selection' section with detailed justification for each criterion, reinstate the 'Regulatory Compliance Integration' section covering AICPA AT-C requirements, add back the 'Management Assertion Development' framework, and then continue with comprehensive sections on: (1) Governance Domain Maturity Assessment covering all 8+ domains with specific gap analysis, (2) Policy Framework Design with 15+ startup-appropriate policies, and (3) Change Management Capability Assessment with implementation roadmap and training requirements. Keep the enhanced technical scope details but subordinate them to the governance framework structure.

REQUIRED FIXES (ordered by point impact):

  FIX 1: control_remediation_coherence (current: 0%, 6.0 pts at stake)
    Scorer's critique: Score 0% below 75% threshold
    Measurement spec: Evaluates the internal consistency between identified control gaps, proposed remediation priorities, and timeline feasibility, ensuring the gap analysis provides a coherent and executable implementation roadmap.
    Evidence found: (no detailed critique from scorer)
    → ACTION: Improve control_remediation_coherence to meet: Evaluates the internal consistency between identified control gaps, proposed remediation priorities, and timeline feasibility, ensuring the gap analysis provides a coherent and executable implementati

  FIX 2: evidence_control_integration (current: 0%, 5.0 pts at stake)
    Scorer's critique: Score 0% below 75% threshold
    Measurement spec: Assesses the integration between control design recommendations and evidence collection framework, ensuring proposed controls generate appropriate evidence for continuous monitoring and Type II examination testing requirements.
    Evidence found: (no detailed critique from scorer)
    → ACTION: Improve evidence_control_integration to meet: Assesses the integration between control design recommendations and evidence collection framework, ensuring proposed controls generate appropriate evidence for continuous monitoring and Type II examin

  FIX 3: organizational_vendor_alignment (current: 0%, 4.0 pts at stake)
    Scorer's critique: Score 0% below 75% threshold
    Measurement spec: Evaluates the coherence between organizational readiness assessment and vendor risk management approach, ensuring vendor management capabilities align with identified organizational maturity and resource constraints.
    Evidence found: (no detailed critique from scorer)
    → ACTION: Improve organizational_vendor_alignment to meet: Evaluates the coherence between organizational readiness assessment and vendor risk management approach, ensuring vendor management capabilities align with identified organizational maturity and resou

  FIX 4: soc2_scope_precision (current: 0%, 4.0 pts at stake)
    Scorer's critique: Score 0% below 75% threshold
    Measurement spec: Evaluates the precision and completeness of SOC 2 scope definition, ensuring all relevant systems, processes, and business units are properly identified and scoped to avoid delays during examination
    Evidence found: (no detailed critique from scorer)
    → ACTION: Improve soc2_scope_precision to meet: Evaluates the precision and completeness of SOC 2 scope definition, ensuring all relevant systems, processes, and business units are properly identified and scoped to avoid delays during examination

  FIX 5: vulnerability_management_completeness (current: 0%, 4.0 pts at stake)
    Scorer's critique: Score 0% below 75% threshold
    Measurement spec: Assesses the completeness of vulnerability management processes including scanning frequency, remediation procedures, and security patching documentation
    Evidence found: (no detailed critique from scorer)
    → ACTION: Improve vulnerability_management_completeness to meet: Assesses the completeness of vulnerability management processes including scanning frequency, remediation procedures, and security patching documentation

  FIX 6: change_management_formality (current: 0%, 4.0 pts at stake)
    Scorer's critique: Score 0% below 75% threshold
    Measurement spec: Assesses the formality and consistency of change management processes including testing procedures, approval workflows, and documentation requirements for production systems
    Evidence found: (no detailed critique from scorer)
    → ACTION: Improve change_management_formality to meet: Assesses the formality and consistency of change management processes including testing procedures, approval workflows, and documentation requirements for production systems

  FIX 7: tsc_mapping_precision.granular_mapping_accuracy (current: 75%, 2.8 pts at stake)
    Scorer's critique: Content demonstrates strong TSC mapping with specific point of focus references and comprehensive coverage of all 64 Common Criteria points. Goes beyond basic requirements with detailed hierarchical mapping.
    Measurement spec: 1.0 if maps to 85%+ of applicable points of focus with <5% mapping errors, 0.7 if maps to 70-84% with <10% errors, 0.4 if maps to 50-69% or has 10-20% mapping errors, 0.0 if <50% coverage or >20% errors
    → ACTION: Minor improvement: Include more specific gap mappings to individual points of focus rather than ranges.

  FIX 8: tsc_mapping_precision.common_criteria_completeness (current: 75%, 2.4 pts at stake)
    Scorer's critique: Excellent coverage of all Common Criteria with specific point of focus mapping and startup-appropriate adaptations. Demonstrates deep understanding of TSC framework structure.
    Measurement spec: 1.0 if addresses all 64 CC points of focus with org-size appropriate detail, 0.7 if covers 55-63 points with minor gaps, 0.4 if covers 40-54 points with notable omissions, 0.0 if <40 points covered
    → ACTION: Consider adding more specific control examples for each point of focus to reach full completeness.

  FIX 9: timeline_resource_feasibility.phase_timeline_accuracy (current: 25%, 2.4 pts at stake)
    Scorer's critique: Individual control timelines provided but missing comprehensive SOC 2 examination timeline with description and testing periods.
    Measurement spec: 1.0 if provides detailed month-by-month timeline accounting for 3-6 month description period, 6-12 month testing period, and auditor busy season constraints, 0.7 if good timeline with minor gaps, 0.4 if basic timeline missing key phases, 0.0 if unrealistic or incomplete timeline
    Evidence found: Provides month-by-month timeline for 12-18 months: not found in content; Accounts for 3-6 month description period: not found in content; Includes 6-12 month testing period: not found in content; Considers auditor busy season constraints: not found in content; Goes beyond rubric guidance with independent domain expertise: No comprehensive timeline provided
    ✗ FAILED CHECK: Provides month-by-month timeline for 12-18 months
      Evidence: not found in content
    ✗ FAILED CHECK: Accounts for 3-6 month description period
      Evidence: not found in content
    ✗ FAILED CHECK: Includes 6-12 month testing period
      Evidence: not found in content
    ✗ FAILED CHECK: Considers auditor busy season constraints
      Evidence: not found in content
    ✗ FAILED CHECK: Goes beyond rubric guidance with independent domain expertise
      Evidence: No comprehensive timeline provided
    → ACTION: Add master timeline showing: Months 1-4 (description period), Months 5-12 (testing period), avoiding Jan-Apr auditor busy season, with monthly milestones.

  FIX 10: risk_impact_prioritization.quantitative_risk_methodology (current: 75%, 2.4 pts at stake)
    Scorer's critique: Strong quantitative methodology with 4 weighted factors but needs application to more identified gaps to meet 20+ requirement.
    Measurement spec: 1.0 if uses 5+ weighted factors (business impact, implementation complexity, auditor focus, customer requirements, regulatory impact) with numerical scoring model, 0.7 if uses 3-4 factors with quantification, 0.4 if basic scoring with 2 factors, 0.0 if qualitative-only or no systematic approach
    Evidence found: Applies systematic scoring to 20+ gaps: Only 6 specific risk scores provided
    ✗ FAILED CHECK: Applies systematic scoring to 20+ gaps
      Evidence: Only 6 specific risk scores provided
    → ACTION: Apply the established 4-factor risk scoring methodology to all identified control gaps, not just the high-priority ones.

  FIX 11: control_gap_prioritization.risk_based_prioritization (current: 75%, 2.4 pts at stake)
    Scorer's critique: Excellent quantitative risk scoring methodology with 4 weighted factors and consistent numerical application across multiple gaps. Demonstrates advanced prioritization approach.
    Measurement spec: 1.0 if uses 4+ weighted risk factors (audit materiality, implementation complexity, timeline constraints, business impact) with numerical scoring, 0.7 if uses 3 factors with some quantification, 0.4 if basic prioritization with limited factors, 0.0 if no systematic prioritization approach
    → ACTION: Consider expanding to include more medium and low priority gaps to reach the full 12+ high-priority threshold.

  FIX 12: vendor_risk_mapping.vendor_inventory_comprehensiveness (current: 75%, 2.4 pts at stake)
    Scorer's critique: Excellent vendor inventory with 32 vendors mapped across all functions with detailed risk categorization. Exceeds minimum requirements.
    Measurement spec: 1.0 if maps 25+ vendors with detailed risk categorization across technology/business/admin functions, 0.7 if maps 20-24 vendors with good categorization, 0.4 if maps 15-19 vendors with basic categorization, 0.0 if <15 vendors or inadequate categorization
    → ACTION: Consider adding vendor dependency impact analysis to further enhance the comprehensive mapping.

  FIX 13: timeline_resource_feasibility.resource_cashflow_alignment (current: 50%, 2.1 pts at stake)
    Scorer's critique: Good cost awareness with specific ranges but lacks phased budget structure aligned with startup cash flow cycles.
    Measurement spec: 1.0 if explicitly addresses startup cash flow timing with phased cost structure and budget optimization, 0.7 if some budget considerations, 0.4 if basic cost awareness, 0.0 if no cash flow considerations
    Evidence found: Provides phased cost structure: Individual costs provided but no phased budget structure; Goes beyond rubric guidance with independent domain expertise: Basic cost awareness without comprehensive cash flow planning
    ✗ FAILED CHECK: Provides phased cost structure
      Evidence: Individual costs provided but no phased budget structure
    ✗ FAILED CHECK: Goes beyond rubric guidance with independent domain expertise
      Evidence: Basic cost awareness without comprehensive cash flow planning
    → ACTION: Create quarterly budget allocation showing costs spread over 12-18 months aligned with typical Series A funding cycles.

  FIX 14: risk_impact_prioritization.remediation_sequencing (current: 50%, 2.1 pts at stake)
    Scorer's critique: Good resource allocation considerations but lacks comprehensive remediation roadmap with dependency mapping.
    Measurement spec: 1.0 if provides detailed remediation roadmap with dependency mapping and resource allocation for 50-person org, 0.7 if good sequencing with minor gaps, 0.4 if basic sequencing without dependencies, 0.0 if no logical sequencing approach
    Evidence found: Provides detailed remediation roadmap with dependencies: Individual timelines provided but no comprehensive roadmap found; Maps dependencies between controls: not found in content; Goes beyond rubric guidance with independent domain expertise: Basic sequencing without comprehensive dependency mapping
    ✗ FAILED CHECK: Provides detailed remediation roadmap with dependencies
      Evidence: Individual timelines provided but no comprehensive roadmap found
    ✗ FAILED CHECK: Maps dependencies between controls
      Evidence: not found in content
    ✗ FAILED CHECK: Goes beyond rubric guidance with independent domain expertise
      Evidence: Basic sequencing without comprehensive dependency mapping
    → ACTION: Create a master remediation roadmap showing dependencies between controls (e.g., governance structure before risk assessment, access controls before monitoring).

  FIX 15: control_gap_prioritization.timeline_realism (current: 50%, 2.1 pts at stake)
    Scorer's critique: Provides some realistic timelines for individual controls but lacks comprehensive SOC 2 examination timeline including description and testing periods.
    Measurement spec: 1.0 if timeline accounts for 3-6 month description period, 6-12 month testing period, auditor availability constraints, and realistic resource allocation for 50-person org, 0.7 if mostly realistic with minor timing issues, 0.4 if unrealistic expectations or missing key timeline factors, 0.0 if funda
    Evidence found: Accounts for 3-6 month description period: not found in content; Includes 6-12 month testing period considerations: not found in content; Considers auditor availability constraints: not found in content; Goes beyond rubric guidance with independent domain expertise: Basic timeline estimates without SOC 2 examination phase considerations
    ✗ FAILED CHECK: Accounts for 3-6 month description period
      Evidence: not found in content
    ✗ FAILED CHECK: Includes 6-12 month testing period considerations
      Evidence: not found in content
    ✗ FAILED CHECK: Considers auditor availability constraints
      Evidence: not found in content
    ✗ FAILED CHECK: Goes beyond rubric guidance with independent domain expertise
      Evidence: Basic timeline estimates without SOC 2 examination phase considerations
    → ACTION: Add overall SOC 2 timeline showing 3-6 month description period, 6-12 month testing period, and auditor busy season considerations (Jan-Apr avoidance).

  FIX 16: vendor_risk_mapping.soc_report_collection_strategy (current: 75%, 2.1 pts at stake)
    Scorer's critique: Strong SOC 2 collection strategy with carve-out approach and alternative assessments for non-SOC vendors. Good strategic thinking.
    Measurement spec: 1.0 if identifies 10+ high-risk vendors requiring SOC 2 reports with alternative assessment procedures for non-SOC vendors, 0.7 if identifies 7-9 high-risk vendors with collection strategy, 0.4 if identifies 4-6 vendors with basic strategy, 0.0 if <4 vendors or no collection strategy
    → ACTION: Expand to identify 2 more high-risk vendors to reach the full 10+ threshold for maximum score.

  FIX 17: control_design_sod_matrix.authorization_matrix_design (current: 75%, 2.1 pts at stake)
    Scorer's critique: Comprehensive authorization matrix with detailed role definitions and appropriate segregation for 50-person organization. Demonstrates advanced understanding of access control design.
    Measurement spec: 1.0 if provides detailed authorization matrices for all privileged functions with appropriate role segregation for org size, 0.7 if covers most functions with minor gaps, 0.4 if basic matrices with significant gaps, 0.0 if inadequate or missing authorization structure
    → ACTION: Consider adding more specific approval workflows and time-limited access procedures for emergency scenarios.

  FIX 18: governance_maturity_assessment.governance_domain_coverage (current: 50%, 2.0 pts at stake)
    Scorer's critique: Good governance domain coverage with specific gap identification but lacks comprehensive maturity assessment framework.
    Measurement spec: 1.0 if assesses 8+ governance domains with specific maturity gaps and remediation requirements, 0.7 if assesses 6-7 domains with good analysis, 0.4 if assesses 4-5 domains with basic analysis, 0.0 if <4 domains or inadequate analysis
    Evidence found: Goes beyond rubric guidance with independent domain expertise: Standard governance assessment without advanced maturity framework
    ✗ FAILED CHECK: Goes beyond rubric guidance with independent domain expertise
      Evidence: Standard governance assessment without advanced maturity framework
    → ACTION: Add maturity scoring (1-5 scale) for each governance domain and detailed gap analysis with specific remediation timelines.

  FIX 19: control_operational_effectiveness.automation_identification (current: 50%, 2.0 pts at stake)
    Scorer's critique: Some automation identification with specific tool recommendations but lacks comprehensive coverage of 60%+ of manual controls.
    Measurement spec: 1.0 if identifies automation for 60%+ of manual controls with specific tooling recommendations, 0.7 if identifies 40-59% with good recommendations, 0.4 if identifies 20-39% with basic recommendations, 0.0 if <20% automation coverage
    Evidence found: Identifies automation for 60%+ of manual controls: Limited automation examples found - mainly deployment pipeline and access reviews; Covers 25+ key controls for automation assessment: not found in content; Goes beyond rubric guidance with independent domain expertise: Basic automation identification without comprehensive coverage
    ✗ FAILED CHECK: Identifies automation for 60%+ of manual controls
      Evidence: Limited automation examples found - mainly deployment pipeline and access reviews
    ✗ FAILED CHECK: Covers 25+ key controls for automation assessment
      Evidence: not found in content
    ✗ FAILED CHECK: Goes beyond rubric guidance with independent domain expertise
      Evidence: Basic automation identification without comprehensive coverage
    → ACTION: Expand automation analysis to cover vulnerability scanning, compliance monitoring, incident response, and backup verification processes with specific tool recommendations.

  FIX 20: due_diligence_procedures.risk_tiered_procedures (current: 75%, 2.0 pts at stake)
    Scorer's critique: Excellent risk-tiered due diligence procedures with specific requirements and frequencies for each of three risk tiers.
    Measurement spec: 1.0 if designs comprehensive procedures for 3+ risk tiers with specific requirements for each tier, 0.7 if designs procedures for 2-3 tiers with good detail, 0.4 if basic tiered approach, 0.0 if single-tier or no tiered procedures
    → ACTION: Consider adding specific escalation procedures for vendors that fail to meet due diligence requirements.

  FIX 21: governance_maturity_assessment.policy_framework_design (current: 25%, 1.8 pts at stake)
    Scorer's critique: Minimal policy framework design provided - lacks comprehensive policy inventory and startup-appropriate framework.
    Measurement spec: 1.0 if designs comprehensive policy framework with 15+ policies appropriate for startup scale and growth trajectory, 0.7 if designs 10-14 policies with good startup considerations, 0.4 if designs 5-9 policies with basic framework, 0.0 if <5 policies or inappropriate scope
    Evidence found: Designs 15+ policies appropriate for startup scale: Limited policy framework discussion found; Addresses scalability considerations: not found in content; Provides comprehensive framework: not found in content; Goes beyond rubric guidance with independent domain expertise: Minimal policy framework design
    ✗ FAILED CHECK: Designs 15+ policies appropriate for startup scale
      Evidence: Limited policy framework discussion found
    ✗ FAILED CHECK: Addresses scalability considerations
      Evidence: not found in content
    ✗ FAILED CHECK: Provides comprehensive framework
      Evidence: not found in content
    ✗ FAILED CHECK: Goes beyond rubric guidance with independent domain expertise
      Evidence: Minimal policy framework design
    → ACTION: Add complete policy framework including: Information Security Policy, Access Control Policy, Change Management Policy, Incident Response Policy, Vendor Management Policy, Data Classification Policy, etc.

  FIX 22: control_operational_effectiveness.exception_handling_design (current: 50%, 1.8 pts at stake)
    Scorer's critique: Good exception handling for security incidents but lacks comprehensive exception procedures across all critical control areas.
    Measurement spec: 1.0 if provides comprehensive exception procedures for all critical controls with escalation paths, 0.7 if covers most controls with minor gaps, 0.4 if basic exception handling with some gaps, 0.0 if inadequate exception procedures
    Evidence found: Covers all critical controls: Only security incident exception handling found, missing other control areas; Goes beyond rubric guidance with independent domain expertise: Basic exception handling without comprehensive coverage
    ✗ FAILED CHECK: Covers all critical controls
      Evidence: Only security incident exception handling found, missing other control areas
    ✗ FAILED CHECK: Goes beyond rubric guidance with independent domain expertise
      Evidence: Basic exception handling without comprehensive coverage
    → ACTION: Add exception handling procedures for access control failures, change management emergencies, vendor outages, and backup restoration scenarios.

  FIX 23: due_diligence_procedures.alternative_assessment_methods (current: 50%, 1.8 pts at stake)
    Scorer's critique: Good alternative assessment procedures with questionnaires but lacks comprehensive methods like penetration testing or on-site reviews.
    Measurement spec: 1.0 if provides detailed alternative assessment procedures including questionnaires, penetration testing, on-site reviews for non-SOC vendors, 0.7 if some alternative procedures, 0.4 if basic alternative assessment, 0.0 if no alternative procedures
    Evidence found: Addresses penetration testing or on-site reviews: not found in content; Goes beyond rubric guidance with independent domain expertise: Basic alternative assessment without comprehensive methods
    ✗ FAILED CHECK: Addresses penetration testing or on-site reviews
      Evidence: not found in content
    ✗ FAILED CHECK: Goes beyond rubric guidance with independent domain expertise
      Evidence: Basic alternative assessment without comprehensive methods
    → ACTION: Add penetration testing requirements for critical vendors without SOC 2 reports and on-site assessment procedures for high-risk vendors.

  FIX 24: tsc_mapping_precision.additional_criteria_alignment (current: 50%, 1.8 pts at stake)
    Scorer's critique: Good identification and justification of additional criteria but lacks detailed mapping of points of focus for the selected additional criteria.
    Measurement spec: 1.0 if correctly identifies all applicable additional criteria with justification and maps 80%+ of points of focus, 0.7 if identifies criteria but incomplete mapping (60-79%), 0.4 if partial identification or surface-level mapping, 0.0 if incorrect criteria selection
    Evidence found: Maps 80%+ of points of focus for selected additional criteria: not found in content; Goes beyond rubric guidance with independent domain expertise: Standard criteria selection without advanced analysis
    ✗ FAILED CHECK: Maps 80%+ of points of focus for selected additional criteria
      Evidence: not found in content
    ✗ FAILED CHECK: Goes beyond rubric guidance with independent domain expertise
      Evidence: Standard criteria selection without advanced analysis
    → ACTION: Add specific point of focus mapping for Availability, Processing Integrity, Confidentiality, and Privacy criteria similar to the Common Criteria mapping provided.

  FIX 25: auditor_selection_timing.auditor_category_analysis (current: 0%, 1.6 pts at stake)
    Scorer's critique: No auditor selection analysis provided in the content.
    Measurement spec: 1.0 if analyzes Big Four, national, and regional firms with specific cost ranges and market credibility implications, 0.7 if analyzes 2 categories with good detail, 0.4 if basic auditor comparison, 0.0 if inadequate auditor analysis
    Evidence found: Analyzes Big Four, national, and regional firms: not found in content; Provides specific cost ranges: not found in content; Addresses market credibility implications: not found in content; Goes beyond rubric guidance with independent domain expertise: No auditor selection analysis provided
    ✗ FAILED CHECK: Analyzes Big Four, national, and regional firms
      Evidence: not found in content
    ✗ FAILED CHECK: Provides specific cost ranges
      Evidence: not found in content
    ✗ FAILED CHECK: Addresses market credibility implications
